questions.text: The process of doping causes the resistivity of semi conductor to:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Doping introduces impurities into a semiconductor, which can increase the number of charge carriers (either electrons or holes) available for conduction. This increase in charge carriers results in a decrease in resistivity, as there are more carriers available to carry current through the material.
Doping alters the semiconductor's properties, so its resistivity typically changes rather than remaining constant.
This is incorrect because doping typically decreases the resistivity by increasing the number of charge carriers available for conduction.

questions.explanation: The purpose of doping is to increase the number of free electrons or holes in the semiconductor crystal. It increases the electrical conductivity of the semiconductor. In other words it decreases the resistance of the semiconductor﻿Due to doping of pure semiconductor with trivalent or pentavalent atoms, number density of majority charge carriers increases which increases the conductivity of semiconductor and hence decreases its resistivity
hints: causes the resistivity of semi conductor to

questions.text: An n-type substance is:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: p-type and n-type semiconductors are formed when impurity atoms, which are themselves electrically neutral, are added to a pure semiconductor that is also electrically neutral. Therefore, the p- or n-type semiconductor formed is electrically neutral.

questions.text: Holes exist in:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: There is no hole current in conductorsbecause they have overlappingvalence and conduction bands. In conductors, electrons are loosely bound to the nucleus hence, can detach easily at room temperature. Also, a large number of free electrons thus, available are conduction electrons

Holes are formed when electrons in atoms move out of the valence band (the outermost shell of the atom that is completely filled with electrons) into the conduction band (the area in an atom where electrons can escape easily), which happens everywhere in a semiconductor.
Holes donot exist in insulators as they donot have free moving electrons
Holes donot exist in conductors or Insulators.

questions.explanation: Holes are formed when electrons in atoms move out of the valence band (the outermost shell of the atom that is completely filled with electrons) into the conduction band (the area in an atom where electrons can escape easily), which happens everywhere in a semiconductor.

questions.text: The crystal of Germinium or silicon in its pure form at absolute zero acts as:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: In silicon, the crystalline form is a silvery, metallic-looking substance. In a silicon lattice, all silicon atoms bond perfectly to four neighbors, leaving no free electrons to conduct electric current. This makes a silicon crystal an insulator rather than a conductor.In semiconductors i.e. germanium the energy gap is small. Hence at room temperatures some of the valence electrons gain enough thermal energy and jump to the conduction band. More the thermal energy available, the more electrons will get excited and jump to the conduction band. According to the Boltzmann law, the number of thermally excited electrons (n )is given by,n∝e−Eg/KT where Eg is the energy gap for a particular substance, T is the temperature of the substance and K is the Boltzmann constant.If we consider the above equation the number of thermally excited electrons keeps on increasing as we increase the temperature T. If the temperature of the semiconductor is brought down to 0K then the number of thermally excited electrons will literally be nil. Hence there won’t be any electrons in the valence band. Hence we can conclude that at 0K, germanium will behave as an insulator.

questions.text: All the valence electrons present in a crystal of silicon are bounded in their orbits by:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In a crystal of silicon, each silicon atom forms four covalent bonds with neighboring silicon atoms, creating a crystalline lattice structure. The valence electrons are shared in these covalent bonds, which keep them bounded in their orbits within the crystal lattice.

Ionic bonds involve the transfer of electrons from one atom to another, resulting in the formation of positively and negatively charged ions that are held together by electrostatic attraction. Silicon does not typically form ionic bonds in its crystal structure; instead, it forms covalent bonds.

Molecular bonds involve the sharing of electrons between atoms to form molecules. Silicon does not exist as discrete molecules in a crystal; rather, it forms a continuous lattice structure held together by covalent bonds.
This option is incorrect because silicon in its crystalline form primarily exhibits covalent bonding to hold its structure together.

questions.explanation: As silicon is a non metal all of its valence electrons will form covalent bonds by sharing if electrons to fill their outermost shells and gain stability.

questions.text: While drawing a graph between current and biasing voltage in p-n junction the current is taken:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because the x-axis usually represents the biasing voltage (or potential difference), not the current.

This option is incorrect because it's uncommon to plot any physical quantity along the negative y-axis in standard graphs. Additionally, in the context of p-n junctions, current is typically positive and flows in one direction.
This option is correct. The x-axis represents the biasing voltage, and the current is plotted along the y-axis. Expressing current in mA (milliamps) is common for clarity and practicality.

This option is incorrect because it's unusual to plot data along the positive y-axis only. Typically, positive values are plotted above the x-axis, but the current is generally plotted above and below the x-axis to represent both positive and negative values.

questions.explanation: The x-axis represents the biasing voltage, and the current is plotted along the y-axis. Expressing current in mA (milliamps) is common for clarity and practicality.

questions.text: The semi conductor diode has the property of:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Semiconductor diodes allow current to flow in only one direction, from the anode to the cathode, making them capable of one-way conduction. They do not conduct in both directions.

This option is incorrect. Semiconductor diodes, when forward-biased (voltage applied in the correct direction), allow current to flow. When reverse-biased (voltage applied in the opposite direction), they have a small leakage current but are not fully conducting in that direction, so it's not zero conduction.

This option is correct. Semiconductor diodes are primarily used for one-way conduction, allowing current flow in only one direction. This property is essential for rectification, where AC voltage is converted into DC voltage.

This option is incorrect. Semiconductor diodes do not amplify signals. They are primarily used as rectifiers, voltage regulators, and signal demodulators, but they do not provide amplification like transistors or operational amplifiers.

questions.explanation: A semiconductor diode is a device that permits the flow of electric current in only one direction and is made from a silicon or germanium semiconductor material. Semiconductor diode characteristics include unidirectional current flow, a non-linear voltage-current characteristic, and low forward resistance.

questions.text: Electrons present in P-type material due to thermal pair generation are:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Majority carriers are the dominant charge carriers in a semiconductor material. In a p-type semiconductor, the majority carriers are the "holes" created due to the absence of electrons in the valence band. Electrons generated by thermal pair generation in a p-type material are not the majority carriers; hence, option a is incorrect.

Minority carriers are the less abundant charge carriers in a semiconductor material. In a p-type semiconductor, the minority carriers are the electrons present in the conduction band. These electrons are generated by thermal pair generation, where some electron-hole pairs are created due to thermal energy. Since these electrons are less abundant compared to the majority carriers (holes), they are considered minority carriers. Therefore, option b is correct.
There is no concept of "dual carriers" in semiconductor physics. It seems to be a made-up term. Thus, option c is incorrect.
This option is incorrect because option b (minority carriers) is correct.

questions.explanation: Electrons are minority charge carriers in p type semiconductors. In an intrinsic semiconductor, some of the electrons in the valence band gain enough energy (thermal energy) to move to the conduction band. So there are a few electrons in the conduction band, and these electrons leave holes in the valence band as they go to the conduction band.

questions.text: Semi conductors with donor atoms and free electrons belong to the type:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Semiconductors with donor atoms (which donate extra electrons) and free electrons belong to the n-type. In n-type semiconductors, the majority charge carriers are electrons.

Semiconductors with acceptor atoms (which accept electrons creating "holes" or lack of electrons) and free holes belong to the p-type. In p-type semiconductors, the majority charge carriers are holes.
This option is incorrect because semiconductors can either be n-type or p-type, not both simultaneously.

This option is incorrect because the classification of semiconductors as n-type or p-type depends on the type of doping introduced (donor atoms for n-type and acceptor atoms for p-type). They cannot belong to both simultaneously.

questions.explanation: N-type semiconductors are created by doping an intrinsic semiconductor with an electron donor element during manufacture. The term n-type comes from the negative charge of the electron. In n-type semiconductors, electrons are the majority carriers and holes are the minority carriers.

questions.text: P -n junction when reversed biased acts as a:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: When a P-N junction is forward-biased, it acts like a conductor, allowing current to flow easily.When reverse-biased, it doesn't behave like a capacitor. Instead, it blocks the flow of current, unlike a capacitor which stores and releases charge.

A P-N junction, when reversed biased, does not exhibit inductive properties. Inductors store energy in a magnetic field when current flows through them, but the P-N junction in reverse bias does not exhibit such behavior.

This option might seem plausible because a forward-biased P-N junction can act like a closed switch, allowing current to flow. However, in reverse bias, it doesn't function as an "on" switch. It behaves more like an "off" switch because it prevents current flow.

When a P-N junction is reverse biased, it acts as an "off" switch because it effectively blocks the flow of current. This is due to the formation of a depletion region where charge carriers are repelled away from the junction, preventing current flow. Therefore, option d is correct.

questions.explanation: A p-n junction diode acts as an off switch when operating in a reverse-biased region. When a diode or any two-terminal component is connected in a circuit, it has two biased conditions with the given supply: forward bias and reverse bias. In reverse bias, the anode is connected to the negative terminal and the cathode to the positive terminal of the supply. A diode cannot conduct in reverse bias and hence is an open switch.

questions.text: The width of depletion region of a junction:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: When a junction is reverse biased, the depletion region widens. This is because the externally applied voltage pushes the majority of carriers away from the junction, leaving behind immobile ions, resulting in an expanded depletion region.

The width of the depletion region is inversely proportional to the doping concentration. Light doping means there are fewer dopant atoms available to create charge carriers, resulting in a wider depletion region. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

Heavy doping means there are more dopant atoms present, which increases the number of charge carriers available. As a result, the depletion region becomes narrower since there are more carriers to neutralize the ionized dopant atoms. Therefore, this option is incorrect.
As explained above, option (a) is correct, making this option incorrect.

questions.explanation: The depletion region width on each side of the p-n junction inversely varies with the dopant concentration. Thus, the depletion width decreases when the dopant concentration is higher and increases when the dopant concentration is lower.

questions.text: Which one of the following has the greatest energy gap:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: CONCEPT:Conductor: The material that allows the electric current to flow through them easily is called a conductor.Example: Metals like -iron, silver, copper, etc.Insulator: The material that doesn't allow the electric current to flow through them is called an insulator.Example: Rubber, wood, etc.Semiconductor: The material having an electric current conducting tendency between conductor and insulator is called a semiconductor.Example: Silicon, germanium, etc.Valence band: The region in which the valence electrons of the atom move is called the valence band.Conduction band: The region in which free electrons remain is called the conduction band.Band Gap: The distance between the valence band of electrons and the conduction band of electrons is known as bandgap.It represents the minimum energy that is required to excite an electron up in the valance band to a state in the conduction band where it can participate in conduction.Conductors have the least band gapwhile insulators have maximum. Semiconductors have a bandgap between conductors and insulators.EXPLANATION:Insulators have the highest bandgapof order 6eV (- 6eV) or more.Semi-Conductors have a bandgap of the order of 1 eV. Conductors have no energy gap (least energy bandgap).This is the reason, they have high conductivity because electrons can move easily from the valance band to the conduction band. Hence the answer will be insulators

questions.text: Which one of the following has smallest energy gap:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: CONCEPT:Conductor: The material that allows the electric current to flow through them easily is called a conductor.Example: Metals like -iron, silver, copper, etc.Insulator: The material that doesn't allow the electric current to flow through them is called an insulator.Example: Rubber, wood, etc.Semiconductor: The material having an electric current conducting tendency between conductor and insulator is called a semiconductor.Example: Silicon, germanium, etc.Valence band: The region in which the valence electrons of the atom move is called the valence band.Conduction band: The region in which free electrons remain is called the conduction band.Band Gap: The distance between the valence band of electrons and the conduction band of electrons is known as bandgap.It represents the minimum energy that is required to excite an electron up in the valance band to a state in the conduction band where it can participate in conduction.Conductors have the least band gap while insulators have maximum. Semiconductors have a bandgap between conductors and insulators.EXPLANATION:Insulators have the highest bandgapof order 6eV (- 6eV) or more.Semi-Conductors have a bandgap of the order of 1 eV. Conductors have no energy gap (least energy bandgap).This is the reason, they have high conductivity because electrons can move easily from the valance band to the conduction band.So the correct answer is option A.

questions.text: Minority carries in n-type substances are:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: In the n-type semiconductor, electrons are the majority carriers, and holes the minority carriers

questions.text: Minority carriers in a p-type substances are:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: In a p-type semiconductor, the majority carriers are holes, and the minority carriers are electrons

questions.text: A junction between p and n materials forms:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: P-N junction diode is a basic semiconductor device that is widely used in electronic circuits. It is a two-terminal device that allows the flow of current in one direction and blocks it in the opposite direction. The P-N junction diode has many applications, including as a detector, rectifier, and voltage regulator.

questions.text: Semi conductor diode conducts only when it is:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: When the diode is forward-biased, the voltage across the diode is in the direction that allows current to flow through it, and the diode conducts. However, when the diode is reverse-biased, the voltage across the diode is in the opposite direction, and the diode does not conduct

questions.text: The forward current through a semi conductor diode circuit is due to:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: The more abundant charge carriers are called majority carriers, which are primarily responsible for current transport in a piece of semiconductor. In n-type semiconductors they are electrons, while in p-type semiconductors they are holes.

questions.text: In pn junction, p-type end is basically referred as:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: The diode is a device formed from a junction of n-type and p-type semiconductor material. The lead connected to the p-type material is called the anode and the lead connected to the n-type material is the cathode.

questions.text: In half wave rectification the output DC voltage is obtained acrossthe load for:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: The negative halfcycle of input AC
The positive half cycle of input AC

questions.explanation: The half-wave rectifier is a rectifier that allows one-half of the AC voltage signal and blocks the other half, and converts AC voltage to DC voltage, so we obtain the pulsating DC voltage as an output signal. A single diode works for a single-phase supply, but in the case of a three-phase supply, three diodes are needed. Only half of the input waveforms reach the output, so three diodes are needed. By adding more filters in the half-wave rectifier circuit, the rippling of the AC frequency from the output can be reduced.

questions.text: Forward resistance of p-n junction is:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: ⇒ Forward bias is a potential used to reduce the resistance of a PN junction.⇒ A forward biased PN junction has a minimum depletion layer width and junction resistance of few ohms.

questions.text: Reverse resistance of p-n junction is:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: ⇒ Reverse bias is a potential used to increase the resistance of a PN junction A reverse-biased PN junction has a maximum depletion layer width and high resistance.

questions.text: Conversion of alternating current into direct current is called:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: CONCEPT:Rectification: A rectifier is a device that converts alternating current (AC) into a direct current (DC). This process is called rectification.A p-n junction can be used as a rectifier because it permits current in one direction only.Amplification: The process of increasing the amplitude of the sound wave is called amplification.In this process, we generally increase the volume of the sound.EXPLANATION:According to the definition of amplification, we increase the amplitude of the sound wave.When we increase the amplitude than the volume of the sound increases.So we can say that the process of increasing the sound volume is called amplification. As in the case of rectification, the AC current is converted into DC. Hence option 4 is correct.

questions.text: The most commonly used diode for special purpose is:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: Special Purpose P-N Junction Diodes includes:Zener DiodeLight-Emitting DiodePhotodiodeA solar cell or photovoltaic devicesLight-emitting diodes, photodiodes, and photovoltaic devices are known as optoelectronic junction devices. Light-Emitting Diode (LED)Light-Emitting Diode is an electric component that emits light when the electric current passes through it. Light-Emitting Diode (LED) is a specific type of diode having similar characteristics as the p-n junction diode. When electricity passes through the LED, the electrons recombine with holes emitting light in the process.The symbol of the diode is shown below. The diode is encased with a clear cover to view the emitted light. The LED can be seen in the picture below.When the LED is forward biased, the electrons flow from the N-type to the P-type. Holes are moved from p to n layer. Due to forward bias, at the boundary, the concentration of the minority carriers increases. At the junction, majority carriers recombine with excess minority carriers. When the minority carriers recombine with the majority carriers, energy is released in the form of photons. Photons with energy less or equal than the bandgap are emitted. The intensity of the light increases when the current reaches maximum.The VI-characteristic of a LED is as shown in the figure below.LEDs can emit red, green, orange, yellow, and blue light. LEDs are widely used as decorative lamps, used in television remote, optical communication, automotive, and display devices.Light-Emitting Diodes require less power to operate. They adapt to instant on-off switching capability. They are economical and offer long-life durability.PhotodiodesPhotodiodes are special p-n junction diodes operated in reverse bias. They are mainly designed for detecting optical signals. Photodiodes feature a transparent window which allows light to fall on it. Photodiodes are also addressed with various names like a light detector, photodetector, and photo-sensor. Silicon, Germanium and Indium gallium arsenide are some of the photodiode materials. The symbol of the photodiode is shown below.The real-time image of the photodiode is as shown below.﻿Photodiodes are made of photosensitive semiconducting material to absorb the light which falls on it. Photodiodes are operated under reverse bias conditions below the breakdown voltage.When the energy of the photons which strike (hv) is greater than the energy gap (Eg) of the semiconductor material in the photodiode, electron-hole pairs are created near the depletion region.Electrons and holes are separated before they combine in the presence of an electric field. When electric current passes through it, electrons are accumulated in the n-side and holes in the p-side. EMF is generated when electrons and holes move in the photodiode. current flows through the device when external load is connected. The magnitude of the photocurrent generated is directly proportional to the intensity of incident light.In reverse bias condition, as the intensity of the light increases, the current also increases. Photodiodes are used in logic circuits, solar cell panels, detection circuits, character recognition circuits and optical communication. Photodiodes are used in smoke detectors and fire detectors.

questions.text: A diode, which can turn current ON and OFF in nanosecond is called:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: There are two relevant mechanisms:First, the intended one: Minority carriers, generated by light within or close to the p-n junction and therefore depletion region, reach one of the contacts and therefore are lost for additional transport. This is the contribution, which you can measure externally as generated current or voltage.Second: Photo-generated carriers can also recombine due to scattering mechanisms and recombine with a carrier of opposite sign. Again, this particular carrier pair is lost for future transport. Scattering mechanisms could be crystal defects in the semiconductor material, impurities, phonon-assisted scattering due to elevated temperature, etc. Therefore, every material or heterostructure has some characteristic carrier life time, which determines the shut-off behavior in this case.

questions.text: The specialy designed semi conductor diodes used as indicator lamps in electronic circuits are:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: Light emitting diodes are used as indicator lamps.

questions.text: A combination of p-type and N-type substance give rise to:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: When P-type and N-type come into contact, carriers, which are holes and free electrons, are attracted to each other, recombine at the junction of P-type and N-type, and disappear. Because there are no carriers near the junction, it is called a depletion layer, and it becomes the same state as an insulator.A PN junction hence forms.

questions.text: When p-type of p-n-junction connected to positive end and N-type of the junction connected to the negative terminal of battery then the junction is:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: By convention, forward biasing of diode is defined in such a way that the positive terminal of the battery is connected to the anode (p-type crystal) of the diode whereas the negative terminal of the battery is connected to the cathode (n-type crystal) of the diode.Moreover when the diode is forward biased then the potential barrier in depletion layer decreases.

questions.text: The semi conductor diode has the property of a:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: A diode is a circuit element that allows electric current to flow in only one direction, like a one-way valve . A diode is created by joining a p-type semiconductor to an n-type semiconductor. The junction between these materials is called a p-n junction. . The positions of the valence and conduction bands are the same, but the impurity levels are quite different. When a p-n junction is formed, electrons from the conduction band of the n-type material diffuse to the p-side, where they combine with holes in the valence band. This migration of charge leaves positive ionized donor ions on the n-side and negative ionized acceptor ions on the p-side, producing a narrow double layer of charge at the p-njunction called the depletion layer. The electric field associated with the depletion layer prevents further The junction between these materials is called a p-n junction. . The positions of the valence and conduction bands are the same, but the impurity levels are quite different. When a p-n junction is formed, electrons from the conduction band of the n-type material diffuse to the p-side, where they combine with holes in the valence band. This migration of charge leaves positive ionized donor ions on the n-side and negative ionized acceptor ions on the p-side, producing a narrow double layer of charge at the p-njunction called the depletion layer. The electric field associated with the depletion layer prevents further diffusion.

questions.text: If positive terminal of the battery is connected to n-type and negative terminal is connected to p-type then diode is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: By convention, forward biasing of diode is defined in such a way that the positive terminal of the battery is connected to the anode (p-type crystal) of the diode whereas the negative terminal of the battery is connected to the cathode (n-type crystal) of the diode. Moreover when the diode is forward biased then the potential barrier in depletion layer decreases.

If the positive terminal of the battery is connected to the n-type and the negative terminal is connected to the p-type, the diode is said to be reverse biased. In a reverse-biias configuration, the external voltage pulls electrons away from the junction in the n-type and holes away in the p-type, widening the depletion region and preventing current flow (except a very small leakage current).

questions.explanation: If the positive terminal of the battery is connected to the n-type and the negative terminal is connected to the p-type, the diode is said to be reverse biased. In a reverse-biias configuration, the external voltage pulls electrons away from the junction in the n-type and holes away in the p-type, widening the depletion region and preventing current flow (except a very small leakage current).

questions.text: The process due to which current flows only during alternate half cycle is known as:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: For most power applications, half-wave rectification is insufficient for the task. The harmonic content of the rectifier’s output waveform is very large and consequently difficult to filter. Furthermore, the AC power source only supplies power to the load one half every full cycle, meaning that half of its capacity is unused. Half-wave rectification is, however, a very simple way to reduce power to a resistive load. Some two-position lamp dimmer switches apply full AC power to the lamp filament for “full” brightness and then half-wave rectify it for a lesser light output. (figure below)

questions.text: The circuit of full wave rectification consist of:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: Bridge rectifiers are the type of full-wave rectifier that uses four or more diodes in a bridge circuit configuration. Thereby, it can very efficiently convert AC current to DC current. Therefore, the number of diodes found in a bridge rectifier is 4.

questions.text: In order that a transistor acts as switch, a larger positive potential is applied across its:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: Using a transistor as a switch is the simplest application of the device. A transistor can be extensively used for switching operations, either for opening or closing a circuit. On the other hand, the basic concept behind the operation of a transistor as a switch relies on its mode of operation. Generally, the low-voltage DC is turned on or off by transistors in this mode.Both PNP and NPN transistors can be utilised as switches. A basic terminal transistor can be handled differently from a signal amplifier by biasing both NPN and PNP bipolar transistors with an “ON / OFF” static switch. One of the main uses of the transistor to transform a DC signal “ON” or “OFF” is solid-state switches.Some devices, including LEDs, only require several milliamperes of DC voltages at the logical level and can be directly controlled via the logical gate output. High-power devices such as generators, solenoids or lamps usually need more power to use transistor switches than the usual logic gate..

questions.text: The transistor in a circuit basically acts as:

questions.explanation: A current amplifier, an electronic circuit that is used to magnify or increase the magnitude of the current, keeping the voltage component of the input signal unchanged, finally passes this amplified signal to its succeeding circuit.

questions.text: Emitter base junctionis always:

questions.explanation: In baseemitter transistors the current is flowing forward biased, whereas, in collector and emitter transistors the current is passing in reverse biased. Thus, the emitter-base junction is forward biased and collector-base junction is reverse biased

questions.text: Identify which device used the rectification:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Inductors are primarily used in electrical power and electronic devices for these major purposes: Choking, blocking, attenuating, or filtering/smoothing high frequency noise in electrical circuits. Storing and transferring energy in power converters (dc-dc or ac-dc)

The most common use for capacitors is energy storage, power conditioning, electronic noise filtering, remote sensing and signal coupling/decoupling. Due to the fact that capacitors are an important and versatile function to varied applications, they are used in a wide range of industries.

A transistor is a semiconductor device used to amplify or switch electrical signals and power. It is one of the basic building blocks of modern electronics. It is composed of semiconductor material, usually with at least three terminals for connection to an electronic circuit.

questions.explanation: .A p-n junction diode can work as an excellent rectifier since it offers a low resistance for the current to flow when it is forward biased; but a very high resistance when reverse biased. Thus, it allows current through it only in one direction and acts as a rectifier.

questions.text: Which one of the following devices is based on a p-n junction:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: A photodiode contains a p-n junction. It is used to detect light and is operated in the reverse-biased condition. When no light is incident on the junction, the reverse current is almost negligible but when its p-n junction is exposed to light, the reverse current increases with the intensity of light. It is one of the fastest photo-detection devices.

Light-emitting diodes (LED) are made from special semiconductors such as gallium arsenide phosphide in which the potential barrier between p and n sides is such that when an electron combines with a hole during forward bias conduction, a photon of visible light is emitted. They are commonly used as small light sources.

It consists of a thick n-type region covered by a thin p-type layer. When such a p-n junction having no external bias is exposed to light, absorbed photons generate electron-hole pairs. A current flows through the external circuit which is proportional to the intensity of light.
All the above options are examples of devices that make use of p-n junction.

questions.explanation: Photodiode, light emitting diode and photo voltaic cell are all examples of devices in which a p-n junction is used.

questions.text: Light emitting diode is based on:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Emission of energy in the form of photons
options.explanation: A light-emitting diode is made from special semiconductors such as gallium arsenide and gallium arsenide phosphide in which the potential barrier between p and n sides is such that when an electron combines with a hole during forward bias condition, a photon of visible light is emitted.
Faraday's law is related to electromagnetism. It has no role in the working of a light-emitting diode.

options.text: Ionic bonding between p-type and n-type substances
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because there is no direct 'ionic bonding' between p-type and n-type substances. However, the interaction between the excess electrons in the n-type material and the holes in the p-type material is crucial for the operation of semiconductor devices such as diodes and transistors.

A light-emitting diode is made from special semiconductors such as gallium arsenide and gallium arsenide phosphide in which the potential barrier between p and n sides is such that when an electron combines with a hole during forward bias condition, a photon of visible light is emitted.

questions.explanation: A light-emitting diode is made from special semiconductors such as gallium arsenide and gallium arsenide phosphide in which the potential barrier between p and n sides is such that when an electron combines with a hole during forward bias condition, a photon of visible light is emitted.

questions.text: What is the current flowing in R2 in the circuit shown in Fig.? Given: R1 = 500Ω and R2 = 1kΩ .
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is incorrect because a non-zero voltage across R2 will result in a non-zero current according to Ohm's Law.
Correct! Using Ohm's Law, the voltage across R2 is 5V, leading to a current of 5mA.
This is incorrect; the calculated current using the given values does not match.
This is incorrect as it does not align with the current calculation using Ohm's Law.

questions.explanation: In a parallel circuit, the voltage across all components is the same. Given that the voltage across R2 is 5V, we apply Ohm's Law (V=IR) to find the current: I = V/R = 5V / 1000Ω = 0.005A or 5mA. Therefore, the correct answer is 5mA. The other options do not match the calculations based on the given resistance and voltage.

questions.text: In a bridge full wave rectifier, the input sine wave is 250sin100πt.The output ripple frequency will be:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This frequency would be correct for a half-wave rectifier, where the output ripple frequency matches the input frequency. However, this is not applicable for a full wave rectifier.
This is the correct option. In a full wave rectifier, the ripple frequency is twice the input frequency. Given the input frequency is 50Hz, the output ripple frequency is 100Hz.
This would be incorrect, as it suggests a misunderstanding of the relationship between input and output frequencies in full wave rectification.
This value is incorrect. It is unrelated to the full wave rectification process, which doubles the input frequency.

questions.explanation: The input signal is given as 250sin100πt. The angular frequency (ω) here is 100π, which corresponds to a frequency (f) of 50Hz (since ω = 2πf). In a bridge full wave rectifier, the output ripple frequency is twice the input frequency. Thus, 2 x 50Hz = 100Hz. Therefore, the correct answer is 100Hz. Options 50Hz and 25Hz are incorrect as they do not reflect the doubling of frequency in full wave rectification. 200Hz is incorrect because it overestimates the ripple frequency.

questions.text: When an impurity is doped into an intrinsic semiconductor, the conductivity of the semiconductor:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Doping introduces extra charge carriers or creates mobile charge vacancies, thereby increasing the conductivity of the semiconductor.
This is incorrect. Doping is specifically done to increase conductivity by adding more charge carriers to the semiconductor.
This is incorrect. The purpose of doping is to change the conductivity, specifically to increase it, by adding impurities.
This is incorrect. Doping does not reduce conductivity to zero; it enhances conductivity by introducing additional charge carriers.

questions.explanation: When an impurity is doped into an intrinsic semiconductor, it significantly alters the semiconductor's conductivity. Doping involves adding impurities to the semiconductor material to increase its electrical conductivity. The type of impurity determines the increase in conductivity:N-Type Semiconductor: Adding donor atoms from Group V (such as phosphorus) introduces extra electrons, which enhances electron conductivity.P-Type Semiconductor: Adding acceptor atoms from Group III (such as boron) creates holes, which enhances hole conductivity.Both methods effectively increase the number of charge carriers in the semiconductor, thereby increasing its overall conductivity. Therefore, the correct answer is that the conductivity Increases. The other options are incorrect because they do not align with the purpose and effect of doping in semiconductors.

questions.text: By increasing the temperature, the conductivity of the semiconductor is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In semiconductors, increasing temperature typically decreases resistance, leading to increased conductivity, not decreased.
As temperature increases, more electrons gain enough energy to participate in conduction, thereby increasing the conductivity of the semiconductor.
The conductivity of semiconductors is not constant with temperature changes as thermal energy impacts electron movement.
Conductivity is significantly affected by temperature changes due to the influence on charge carriers in semiconductors.

questions.explanation: In semiconductors, increasing the temperature provides additional energy to electrons, enabling more of them to break free and become charge carriers. This increase in free electrons or holes leads to an increase in conductivity. Therefore, option B is correct as it recognizes this increase in electrical conductivity with temperature.Option A is incorrect because it suggests a decrease in conductivity, which is opposite to what occurs in semiconductors. Option C is incorrect because conductivity does not remain constant with temperature changes. Option D is incorrect because it implies temperature has no effect on conductivity, which is not the case in semiconductors.

questions.text: Which one of the following is an intrinsic semiconductor?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Boron is a group 3 element used as a dopant in semiconductors, making it extrinsic rather than intrinsic.
Copper is a transition metal and a good conductor of electricity, not a semiconductor.
Indium is a group 3 element typically used as a dopant in semiconductors, resulting in extrinsic semiconductors.
Silicon is a group 4 element that can exist as a pure semiconductor, making it an intrinsic semiconductor.

questions.explanation: Intrinsic semiconductors are pure forms of semiconductor materials. Silicon, being a group 4 element, can function as an intrinsic semiconductor without the need for doping. Boron and Indium are group 3 elements and are commonly used as dopants to create extrinsic semiconductors. Copper, on the other hand, is a metal and a good conductor, not a semiconductor.

questions.text: When p-type and N-type semiconductor are combined then we have a:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Incorrect. A resistor is a passive component that opposes the flow of current, not formed by combining semiconductor types.
Incorrect. A capacitor is used to store electrical energy and is not made by combining semiconductors.
Correct. A diode is formed when a P-type and an N-type semiconductor are combined, creating a PN junction that allows current to flow in one direction.
Incorrect. A triode is a type of vacuum tube with three electrodes, not formed by semiconductors alone.

questions.explanation: When P-type and N-type semiconductors are combined, they form a PN junction, which is the fundamental building block of a diode. This junction allows current to flow primarily in one direction, which is the defining characteristic of a diode. A resistor, capacitor, and triode are different types of electronic components with distinct functions and are not formed by combining P-type and N-type semiconductors. Resistors oppose current flow, capacitors store electrical energy, and triodes are vacuum tubes used to amplify signals.

questions.text: One of the most important property of a diode :
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option correctly identifies that a diode allows current to flow in only one direction. This is the essence of rectification, where the diode permits current flow during forward bias and blocks it during reverse bias. This behavior is crucial in converting AC to DC.
This option is incorrect because a diode does not allow current to flow in both directions. It is designed to conduct in one direction only, which is a key property used in rectification processes.

This option is incorrect since diodes are not used as amplifiers. Amplification requires devices such as transistors, which can increase signal strength, whereas diodes are primarily used for controlling current direction.

This option is incorrect because while diodes can be components within oscillator circuits, they do not function as oscillators themselves. Oscillators typically require active components that can produce periodic waveforms.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option A: Allow current in one direction. Diodes are semiconductor devices that exhibit rectification, meaning they allow current to flow in one direction (forward bias) and block it in the opposite direction (reverse bias). This property is fundamental to their use in rectifier circuits, which convert AC to DC electricity. Option B is incorrect because diodes do not conduct current in both directions. Option C is incorrect because diodes are not used as amplifiers; transistors fulfill that role. Option D is incorrect because diodes alone do not function as oscillators, though they may be part of oscillator circuits.

questions.text: The number of diodes used in a bridge rectifier is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: One diode is insufficient for a bridge configuration. A single diode can only provide half-wave rectification, not the full-wave rectification achieved by a bridge rectifier.
Two diodes can create a full-wave rectifier in a center-tap configuration, but not a bridge rectifier. A bridge rectifier specifically requires four diodes to form the bridge configuration.
Three diodes cannot form a complete bridge rectifier. It requires four diodes to allow current flow in both directions of the AC cycle, achieving full-wave rectification.
Four diodes are necessary for a bridge rectifier. This configuration allows for full-wave rectification, converting both halves of the AC waveform into a pulsating DC output.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is option D: Four diodes. A bridge rectifier uses four diodes in a bridge arrangement to convert the entire AC waveform into DC. This setup allows for full-wave rectification, utilizing both the positive and negative halves of the input AC signal. Options A, B, and C are incorrect because using fewer than four diodes cannot achieve the bridge configuration required for full-wave rectification.

questions.text: The electromagnetic waves travel in free space with the speed of _:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Electromagnetic waves travel in free space (like a vacuum) at the speed of light, denoted by the symbol "c." In a vacuum, electromagnetic waves propagate at approximately 299,792,458 (3×10⁸) meters per second, which is the universally constant speed of light.
This option is incorrect according to the explanation given below.
This option is incorrect according to the explanation given below.
As one of the above options is correct, this option is not correct.

questions.explanation: Electromagnetic waves travel in free space (like a vacuum) at the speed of light, denoted by the symbol "c." In a vacuum, electromagnetic waves propagate at approximately 299,792,458 (3×10⁸) meters per second, which is the universally constant speed of light.
